Women in Bahrain acquired voting rights and the right to stand in national elections in the 2002 election.[[235]](#cite_note-237) However, no women were elected to office in that year's polls.[[236]](#cite_note-238) In response to the failure of women candidates, six were appointed to the Shura Council, which also includes representatives of the kingdom's indigenous Jewish and Christian communities.[[237]](#cite_note-239) [Nada Haffadh](/wiki/Nada_Haffadh "Nada Haffadh") became the country's first female cabinet minister on her appointment as Minister of Health in 2004. The quasi-governmental women's group, the [Supreme Council for Women](/wiki/Supreme_Council_for_Women "Supreme Council for Women"), trained female candidates to take part in the 2006 general election. When Bahrain was elected to head the United Nations General Assembly in 2006 it appointed lawyer and women's rights activist [Haya bint Rashid Al Khalifa](/wiki/Haya_Rashed_Al-Khalifa "Haya Rashed Al-Khalifa") President of the United Nations General Assembly, only the third woman in history to head the world body.[[238]](#cite_note-240) Female activist Ghada Jamsheer said "The government used women's rights as a decorative tool on the international level." She referred to the reforms as "artificial and marginal" and accused the government of "hinder[ing] non-governmental women societies".[[174]](#cite_note-GJ_18.12.2006-176)

In 2006, [Lateefa Al Gaood](/wiki/Lateefa_Al_Gaood "Lateefa Al Gaood") became the first female MP after winning by default.[[239]](#cite_note-241) The number rose to four after the 2011 by-elections.[[240]](#cite_note-242) In 2008, [Houda Nonoo](/wiki/Houda_Nonoo "Houda Nonoo") was appointed ambassador to the United States making her the first Jewish ambassador of any Arab country.[[241]](#cite_note-243) In 2011, [Alice Samaan](/wiki/Alees_Samaan "Alees Samaan"), a Christian woman, was appointed ambassador to Britain.[[242]](#cite_note-244)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Media")]

The predominant forms of media in Bahrain consists of weekly and daily newspapers, television, and radio.

Newspapers are widely available in multiple languages such as Arabic, English, Malayalam, etc. to support the varied population. *[Akhbar Al Khaleej](/wiki/Akhbar_Al_Khaleej "Akhbar Al Khaleej")* (أخبار الخليج) and *[Al Ayam](/wiki/Al_Ayam_(Bahrain) "Al Ayam (Bahrain)")* (الأيام) are examples of major Arabic newspapers published daily. *[Gulf Daily News](/wiki/Gulf_Daily_News "Gulf Daily News")* and *[Daily Tribune](/wiki/Daily_Tribune_(Bahrain) "Daily Tribune (Bahrain)")* publish daily newspapers in English. *[Gulf Madhyamam](/wiki/Gulf_Madhyamam "Gulf Madhyamam")* is a newspaper published in Malayalam.

The country's television network operates five networks, all of which are by the [Information Affairs Authority](/wiki/Information_Affairs_Authority "Information Affairs Authority"). Radio, much like the television network, is mostly state-run and usually in Arabic. *Radio Bahrain* is a long-running English language radio station, and *Radio Mirchi 104.2* is a radio station serving the large expatriate population from the Indian subcontinent living in the country.

By June 2012, Bahrain had 961,000 internet users.[[243]](#cite_note-BBC-245) The platform "provides a welcome free space for journalists, although one that is increasingly monitored", according to Reporters Without Borders. Rigorous filtering targets political, human rights, religious material and content deemed obscene. Bloggers and other [netizens](/wiki/Netizens "Netizens") were among those detained during protests in 2011.[[244]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-246)

Bahraini journalists risk prosecution for offences that include "undermining" the government and religion. [Self-censorship](/wiki/Self-censorship "Self-censorship") is widespread. Journalists were targeted by officials during anti-government protests in 2011. Three editors from the now-banned opposition daily [*Al-Wasat*](/wiki/Al-Wasat_(Bahraini_newspaper) "Al-Wasat (Bahraini newspaper)") were sacked and later fined for publishing "false" news. Several foreign correspondents were expelled.[[244]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-246) An independent commission, set up to look into the unrest, found that state media coverage was at times inflammatory. It said opposition groups suffered from lack of access to mainstream media and recommended that the government "consider relaxing censorship". Assessments by Reporters Without Borders have consistently found Bahrain to be one of the most world's most restrictive regimes.[[245]](#cite_note-247)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Bahrain](/wiki/Economy_of_Bahrain "Economy of Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/GDP_per_capita_development_in_Bahrain.svg/330px-GDP_per_capita_development_in_Bahrain.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_in_Bahrain.svg)

GDP per capita development in Bahrain

According to a January 2006 report by the [United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Commission_for_Western_Asia "United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia"), Bahrain has the fastest-growing economy in the Arab world.[[247]](#cite_note-Ref_2008-249) Bahrain's economy is ranked the 55th freest in the 2025 *[Index of Economic Freedom](/wiki/Index_of_Economic_Freedom "Index of Economic Freedom")* and 4th out of 14 countries in the Middle East/North Africa region.

In 2008, Bahrain was named the world's fastest-growing financial centre by the City of London's [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[248]](#cite_note-250)[[249]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-251) Bahrain's banking and financial services sector, particularly [Muslim banking](/wiki/Islamic_banking_and_finance "Islamic banking and finance"), have benefited from the regional boom driven by demand for oil.[[250]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-252) Petroleum production and processing is Bahrain's most exported product, accounting for 60% of export receipts, 70% of government revenues, and 11% of [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product").[[10]](#cite_note-CIA-10) [Aluminium production](/wiki/Aluminium_Bahrain "Aluminium Bahrain") is the second-most exported product, followed by finance and construction materials.[[10]](#cite_note-CIA-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Manama%2C_Bahrain_Decembre_2014.jpg/250px-Manama%2C_Bahrain_Decembre_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manama,_Bahrain_Decembre_2014.jpg)

Manama skyline as viewed from [Juffair](/wiki/Juffair "Juffair")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/The_Avenues_-_Grand_Avenue_section.jpg/250px-The_Avenues_-_Grand_Avenue_section.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Avenues_-_Grand_Avenue_section.jpg)

A view of the Grand Avenue section of the Avenues

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/The_Avenues_-_Outside_view.jpg/250px-The_Avenues_-_Outside_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Avenues_-_Outside_view.jpg)

The view from outside one of the entrance gates at the Avenues

Economic conditions have fluctuated with the changing price of oil since 1985, for example during and following the [Persian Gulf crisis of 1990–91](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"). With its highly developed communication and transport facilities, Bahrain is home to a number of multinational firms and construction proceeds on several major industrial projects. A large share of exports consists of petroleum products made from imported crude oil, which accounted for 51% of the country's imports in 2007.[[146]](#cite_note-TR-148) In October 2008, the Bahraini government introduced a long-term economic vision for Bahrain known as 'Vision 2030' which aims to transform Bahrain into a diversified and sustainable economy.

In recent years, the government has undertaken several economic reforms in order to improve its financial dependency and also to boost its image as an island tourist destination that is compact, has short travel times and provides a much more authentic Arab experience than the regional economic and tourism powerhouse of Dubai.[[251]](#cite_note-253) The Avenues is one such example of the recent developments. It is a waterfront facing shopping mall that was opened in October 2019.[[252]](#cite_note-254) Bahrain depends heavily on food imports to feed its growing population—it imports large amounts of meat and 75% of its fruit.[[253]](#cite_note-255)[[254]](#cite_note-DT-256)

Since only 2.9% of the country's land is [arable](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land"), [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") contributes to 0.5% of Bahrain's GDP.[[254]](#cite_note-DT-256) In 2004, Bahrain signed the [Bahrain–US Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Bahrain%E2%80%93United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "Bahrain–United States Free Trade Agreement"), which will reduce certain trade barriers between the two nations.[[255]](#cite_note-autogenerated8-257) In 2011, due to the combination of the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") and the [2011 Bahraini uprising](/wiki/2011_Bahraini_uprising "2011 Bahraini uprising"), its GDP growth rate decreased to 1.3%, which was the lowest growth rate since 1994.[[256]](#cite_note-258) The country's public debt in 2020 is $44.5 billion, or 130% of GDP. It is expected to rise to 155 per cent of GDP in 2026, according to IMF estimates. The [military expenditure](/wiki/Military_expenditure "Military expenditure") is the main reason for this increase in debt.[[257]](#cite_note-mondediplo1-259)

Access to [biocapacity](/wiki/Biocapacity "Biocapacity") in Bahrain is much lower than the world average. In 2016, Bahrain had 0.52 global hectares[[258]](#cite_note-footprintdata-260) of biocapacity per person within its territory, much less than the world average of 1.6 global hectares per person.[[259]](#cite_note-261)

In 2016, Bahrain used 8.6 global hectares of biocapacity per person – their [ecological footprint](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") of consumption. This means they use 16.5 times as much biocapacity as Bahrain contains. As a result, Bahrain is running a biocapacity deficit.[[258]](#cite_note-footprintdata-260)

Unemployment, especially among the young, and the depletion of both oil and underground water resources are major long-term economic problems. In 2008, the jobless figure was at 4%,[[260]](#cite_note-Ref_2008a-262) with women overrepresented at 85% of the total.[[261]](#cite_note-Ref_2008b-263) In 2007 Bahrain became the first Arab country to institute [unemployment benefits](/wiki/Unemployment_benefits "Unemployment benefits") as part of a series of labour reforms instigated under Minister of Labour, [Majeed Al Alawi](/wiki/Majeed_Al_Alawi "Majeed Al Alawi").[[262]](#cite_note-264)

As of Q2 2023, total employment in Bahrain stood at 770,129 workers[[263]](#cite_note-265). This included both Bahraini and Non-Bahraini workers. These employment levels represented a full recovery of employment since the downturn caused by the COVID pandemic.[[264]](#cite_note-266)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Energy")]

Main article: [Energy in Bahrain](/wiki/Energy_in_Bahrain "Energy in Bahrain")

Bahrain have [energy](/wiki/World_energy_resources_and_consumption "World energy resources and consumption") and [electricity](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation") production, consumption and imports which is making a net energy exporter.

Bahrain was the first place on the Arabian side of the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") where oil was discovered. The [First Oil Well, Bahrain](/wiki/First_Oil_Well,_Bahrain "First Oil Well, Bahrain") situated below [Jabal al Dukhan](/wiki/Mountain_of_Smoke "Mountain of Smoke") has operated since 1932. It was operated by [Bahrain Petroleum Company](/wiki/Bahrain_Petroleum_Company "Bahrain Petroleum Company").

In 2023, the petroleum production of Bahrain was rated at about 189,000 barrels per day.[[265]](#cite_note-267) The petroleum reserves were quoted as 168 million barrels in 2023.[[266]](#cite_note-oilgas2023-268)

As of 2023, Bahrain was said to produce 16.7 billion cubic metres (bcm) of natural gas per year and have reserves of 68 bcm.[[266]](#cite_note-oilgas2023-268)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Bahrain](/wiki/Tourism_in_Bahrain "Tourism in Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Muharraq_and_Manama.jpg/250px-Muharraq_and_Manama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muharraq_and_Manama.jpg)

The cities of [Muharraq](/wiki/Muharraq "Muharraq") (*foreground*) and Manama (background)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Muharraq_Blick_nach_Manama_2.jpg/250px-Muharraq_Blick_nach_Manama_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muharraq_Blick_nach_Manama_2.jpg)

Manama seen from [Muharraq](/wiki/Muharraq "Muharraq")

As a tourist destination, Bahrain received over eleven million visitors in 2019.[[267]](#cite_note-269) Most of these are from the surrounding Arab states, although an increasing number hail from outside the region due to growing awareness of the kingdom's heritage and partly due to its higher profile as a result of the [Bahrain Grand Prix](/wiki/Bahrain_Grand_Prix "Bahrain Grand Prix").

The kingdom combines modern Arab culture and the archaeological legacy of five thousand years of civilisation. The island is home to forts including [Qalat Al Bahrain](/wiki/Qalat_Al_Bahrain "Qalat Al Bahrain") which has been listed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). The [Bahrain National Museum](/wiki/Bahrain_National_Museum "Bahrain National Museum") has artefacts from the country's history dating back to the island's first human inhabitants some 9000 years ago and the [Beit Al Quran](/wiki/Beit_Al_Quran "Beit Al Quran") (Arabic: بيت القرآن, meaning: the House of Qur'an) is a museum that holds Muslim artefacts of the [Qur'an](/wiki/Qur%27an "Qur'an"). Some of the popular historical tourist attractions in the kingdom are the [Al Khamis Mosque](/wiki/Al_Khamis_Mosque "Al Khamis Mosque"), which is one of the oldest mosques in the region, the [Arad fort](/wiki/Arad_fort "Arad fort") in Muharraq, [Barbar temple](/wiki/Barbar_temple "Barbar temple"), which is an ancient temple from the Dilmunite period of Bahrain, as well as the [A'ali Burial Mounds](/wiki/Dilmun_Burial_Mounds "Dilmun Burial Mounds") and the [Saar](/wiki/Sar,_Bahrain "Sar, Bahrain") temple.[[268]](#cite_note-270) The [Tree of Life](/wiki/Tree_of_Life,_Bahrain "Tree of Life, Bahrain"), a 400-year-old tree that grows in the [Sakhir](/wiki/Sakhir "Sakhir") desert with no nearby water, is also a popular tourist attraction.[[269]](#cite_note-271)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Tree_of_Life_Bahrain.jpg/250px-Tree_of_Life_Bahrain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tree_of_Life_Bahrain.jpg)

The [Tree of Life](/wiki/Tree_of_Life,_Bahrain "Tree of Life, Bahrain"), a 9.75 metre high [Prosopis cineraria](/wiki/Prosopis_cineraria "Prosopis cineraria") tree that is over 400 years old

[Bird watching](/wiki/Bird_watching "Bird watching") (primarily in the [Hawar Islands](/wiki/Hawar_Islands "Hawar Islands")), [scuba diving](/wiki/Scuba_diving "Scuba diving"), and [horse riding](/wiki/Horse_riding "Horse riding") are popular tourist activities in Bahrain. Many tourists from nearby Saudi Arabia and across the region visit Manama primarily for the [shopping malls](/wiki/Shopping_malls "Shopping malls") in the capital Manama, such as the [Bahrain City Centre](/wiki/Bahrain_City_Centre "Bahrain City Centre") and [Seef Mall](/wiki/Seef_Mall "Seef Mall") in the [Seef](/wiki/Seef "Seef") district of Manama. The Manama Souq and *Gold Souq* in the old district of Manama are also popular with tourists.[[270]](#cite_note-MoFA-272)

In January 2019 the state-run Bahrain News Agency announced the summer 2019 opening of an underwater theme park covering about 100,000 square metres with a sunken [Boeing 747](/wiki/Boeing_747 "Boeing 747") as the site's centrepiece. The project is a partnership between the Supreme Council for Environment, Bahrain Tourism and Exhibitions Authority (BTEA), and private investors. Bahrain hopes scuba divers from around the world will visit the underwater park, which will also include artificial [coral reefs](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef"), a copy of a Bahraini pearl merchant's house, and sculptures.[[271]](#cite_note-273) The park is intended to become the world's largest eco-friendly underwater theme park.[[272]](#cite_note-274)

Since 2005, Bahrain hosts an annual festival in March, titled *Spring of Culture*, which features internationally renowned musicians and artists performing in concerts.[[273]](#cite_note-TA-275) Manama was named the [Arab Capital of Culture](/wiki/Arab_Capital_of_Culture "Arab Capital of Culture") for 2012 and *Capital of Arab Tourism* for 2013 by the Arab League and Asian Tourism for 2014 with the Gulf Capital of Tourism for 2016 by The Gulf Cooperation Council. The 2012 festival featured concerts starring [Andrea Bocelli](/wiki/Andrea_Bocelli "Andrea Bocelli"), [Julio Iglesias](/wiki/Julio_Iglesias "Julio Iglesias") and other musicians.[[274]](#cite_note-TOB-276)

### Value Added Tax (VAT)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Value Added Tax (VAT)")]

The Kingdom of Bahrain introduced the Value Added Tax with effect from 1 January 2019.[[275]](#cite_note-277) This is a multipoint tax on the sale of goods and services in Kingdom of Bahrain. This has been managed by the government through the national bureau of revenue. The ultimate burden of this tax is passed on the consumer. To start with the maximum rate of VAT was 5% which is increased to 10% with effect from 1 January 2022.[[276]](#cite_note-278) The government of Bahrain is assuring compliance through high penalties on defaults and tighter audits. This first of its kind VAT has invited qualified chartered accounting firms mainly from India to advise on VAT matters. Firms like KPMG, KeyPoint, Assure Consulting and APMH have set up offices looking at the need for consulting in this domain of VAT.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

Main article: [Transport in Bahrain](/wiki/Transport_in_Bahrain "Transport in Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Bahrain_International_Airport_%28New_Terminal%29.jpg/250px-Bahrain_International_Airport_%28New_Terminal%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bahrain_International_Airport_(New_Terminal).jpg)

The new terminal of the Bahrain International Airport

Bahrain has one main [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport"), the [Bahrain International Airport](/wiki/Bahrain_International_Airport "Bahrain International Airport") (BAH) which is located on the island of [Muharraq](/wiki/Muharraq_Island "Muharraq Island"), in the north-east. The airport handled almost 100,000 flights and more than 9.5 million passengers in 2019.[[277]](#cite_note-Stats_2019-279) On 28 January 2021, Bahrain opened its new airport terminal as part of its economic vision 2030.[[278]](#cite_note-english.alarabiya.net-280) The new airport terminal is capable of handling 14 million passengers and is a big boost to the country's aviation sector.[[278]](#cite_note-english.alarabiya.net-280) Bahrain's national carrier, [Gulf Air](/wiki/Gulf_Air "Gulf Air") operates and bases itself in the BIA.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/King_Fahd_causeway_satellite.png/250px-King_Fahd_causeway_satellite.png)](/wiki/File:King_Fahd_causeway_satellite.png)

The King Fahd Causeway as seen from space

Bahrain has a well-developed [road network](/wiki/Road_transport_in_Bahrain "Road transport in Bahrain"), particularly in Manama. The discovery of oil in the early 1930s accelerated the creation of multiple roads and [highways](/wiki/Highways "Highways") in Bahrain, connecting several isolated villages, such as [Budaiya](/wiki/Budaiya "Budaiya"), to Manama.[[279]](#cite_note-Yasser-281)

To the east, a bridge connected Manama to [Muharraq](/wiki/Muharraq_Island "Muharraq Island") since 1929, a new causeway was built in 1941 which replaced the old wooden bridge.[[279]](#cite_note-Yasser-281) Currently there are three modern bridges connecting the two locations.[[280]](#cite_note-282) Transits between the two islands peaked after the construction of the Bahrain International Airport in 1932.[[279]](#cite_note-Yasser-281) [Ring roads](/wiki/Ring_roads "Ring roads") and highways were later built to connect Manama to the villages of the [Northern Governorate](/wiki/Northern_Governorate "Northern Governorate") and towards towns in central and southern Bahrain.

The four main islands and all the towns and villages are linked by well-constructed roads. There were 3,164 km (1,966 mi) of roadways in 2002, of which 2,433 km (1,512 mi) were paved. A [causeway](/wiki/Causeway "Causeway") stretching over 2.8 km (2 mi), connect Manama with [Muharraq Island](/wiki/Muharraq_Island "Muharraq Island"), and another bridge joins [Sitra](/wiki/Sitra "Sitra") to the main island. The [King Fahd Causeway](/wiki/King_Fahd_Causeway "King Fahd Causeway"), measuring 24 km (15 mi), links Bahrain with the Saudi Arabian mainland via the island of [Umm an-Nasan](/wiki/Umm_an-Nasan "Umm an-Nasan"). It was completed in December 1986, and financed by Saudi Arabia. In 2008, there were 17,743,495 passengers transiting through the causeway.[[281]](#cite_note-283) A second causeway, which will have both road and rail connection, between Bahrain and Saudi Arabia called 'King Hamad Causeway' is currently being discussed and is in the planning phase.[[282]](#cite_note-284)

Bahrain's port of [Mina Salman](/wiki/Mina_Salman "Mina Salman") is the main [seaport](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") of the country and consists of 15 [berths](/wiki/Berth_(moorings) "Berth (moorings)").[[283]](#cite_note-autogenerated9-285) In 2001, Bahrain had a merchant fleet of eight ships of 1,000 [GT](/wiki/Gross_Tonnage "Gross Tonnage") or over, totalling 270,784 GT.[[284]](#cite_note-286) Private vehicles and taxis are the primary means of transportation in the city.[[285]](#cite_note-287) A [nationwide metro system](/wiki/Bahrain_Metro "Bahrain Metro") is currently under construction and is due to be operational by 2025.

### Telecommunications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Telecommunications")]

Main articles: [Telecommunications in Bahrain](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Bahrain "Telecommunications in Bahrain") and [Internet in Bahrain](/wiki/Internet_in_Bahrain "Internet in Bahrain")

The [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications") sector in Bahrain officially started in 1981 with the establishment of Bahrain's first telecommunications company, [Batelco](/wiki/Batelco "Batelco") and until 2004, it [monopolised](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly") the sector. In 1981, there were more than 45,000 telephones in use in the country. By 1999, Batelco had more than 100,000 mobile contracts.[[286]](#cite_note-RE-288) In 2002, under pressure from international bodies, Bahrain implemented its telecommunications law which included the establishment of an independent *[Telecommunications Regulatory Authority](/wiki/Telecommunications_Regulatory_Authority_of_Bahrain "Telecommunications Regulatory Authority of Bahrain")* (TRA).[[286]](#cite_note-RE-288) In 2004, [Zain](/wiki/Zain_Group "Zain Group") (a rebranded version of [MTC Vodafone](/wiki/MTC_Vodafone "MTC Vodafone")) started operations in Bahrain and in 2010 VIVA (owned by [STC](/wiki/Saudi_Telecom_Company "Saudi Telecom Company") Group) became the third company to provide mobile services.[[287]](#cite_note-289)

Bahrain has been connected to the internet since 1995 with the country's [domain suffix](/wiki/Domain_suffix "Domain suffix") being '[.bh](/wiki/.bh ".bh")'. The country's connectivity score (a statistic which measures both Internet access and fixed and mobile telephone lines) is 210.4 per cent per person, while the regional average in [Arab States of the Persian Gulf](/wiki/Arab_States_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab States of the Persian Gulf") is 135.37 per cent.[[288]](#cite_note-290) The number of Bahraini [internet users](/wiki/Internet_users "Internet users") has risen from 40,000 in 2000[[289]](#cite_note-291) to 250,000 in 2008,[[290]](#cite_note-292) or from 5.95 to 33 per cent of the population. As of August 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit), the TRA has licensed 22 [Internet service providers](/wiki/Internet_service_provider "Internet service provider").[[291]](#cite_note-293)

## Science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Science and technology")]

### Policy framework

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Policy framework")]

The *Bahraini Economic Vision 2030* published in 2008 does not indicate how the stated goal of shifting from an economy built on oil wealth to a productive, globally competitive economy will be attained. Bahrain has already diversified its exports to some extent, out of necessity. It has the smallest hydrocarbon reserves of any Persian Gulf state, producing 48,000 barrels per day from its one onshore field.[[292]](#cite_note-294) The bulk of the country's revenue comes from its share in the offshore field administered by Saudi Arabia. The gas reserve in Bahrain is expected to last for less than 27 years, leaving the country with few sources of capital to pursue the development of new industries. Investment in research and development remained very low in 2013.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

Apart from the Ministry of Education and the Higher Education Council, the two main hives of activity in science, technology, and innovation are the University of Bahrain (established in 1986) and the Bahrain Centre for Strategic, International, and Energy Studies. The latter was founded in 2009 to undertake research with a focus on strategic security and energy issues to encourage new thinking and influence policymaking.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

### New infrastructure for science and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: New infrastructure for science and education")]

Bahrain hopes to build a science culture within the kingdom and to encourage technological innovation, among other goals. In 2013, the Bahrain Science Centre was launched as an interactive educational facility targeting 6- to 18-year-olds. The topics covered by current exhibitions include junior engineering, human health, the five senses, Earth sciences and biodiversity.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

In April 2014, Bahrain launched its National Space Science Agency. The agency has been working to ratify international space-related agreements such as the [Outer Space Treaty](/wiki/Outer_Space_Treaty "Outer Space Treaty"), the Rescue Agreement, the [Space Liability Convention](/wiki/Space_Liability_Convention "Space Liability Convention"), the Registration Convention and the Moon Agreement. The agency plans to establish infrastructure for the observation of both outer space and the Earth.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

In November 2008, an agreement was signed to establish a Regional Centre for Information and Communication Technology in Manama under the auspices of UNESCO. The aim is to establish a knowledge hub for the six-member states of the Gulf Cooperation Council. In March 2012, the centre hosted two high-level workshops on ICTs and education. In 2013, Bahrain topped the Arab world for internet penetration (90% of the population), trailed by the United Arab Emirates (86%) and Qatar (85%). Just half of Bahrainis and Qataris (53%) and two-thirds of those in the United Arab Emirates (64%) had access in 2009.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

### Investment in education and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Investment in education and research")]

In 2012, the government devoted 2.6% of GDP to education, one of the lowest ratios in the Arab world. This ratio was on a par with investment in education in Lebanon and higher only than that in Qatar (2.4% in 2008) and Sudan (2.2% in 2009).[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295) Bahrain was ranked 72nd in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2024,[[294]](#cite_note-296) but jumped to the 62nd rank according to GII 2025.[[295]](#cite_note-297)
Bahrain invests little in research and development. In 2009 and 2013, this investment reportedly amounted to 0.04% of GDP, although the data were incomplete, covering only the higher education sector. The lack of comprehensive data on research and development poses a challenge for policymakers, as data inform evidence-based policymaking.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

The available data for researchers in 2013 cover only the higher education sector. Here, the number of researchers is equivalent to 50 per million inhabitants, compared to a global average for all employment sectors of 1,083 per million.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

The [University of Bahrain](/wiki/University_of_Bahrain "University of Bahrain") had over 20,000 students in 2014, 65% of whom are women, and around 900 faculty members, 40% of whom are women. From 1986 to 2014, university staff published 5,500 papers and books. The university spent about US$11 million per year on research in 2014, which was conducted by a contingent of 172 men and 128 women. Women thus made up 43% of researchers at the [University of Bahrain](/wiki/University_of_Bahrain "University of Bahrain") in 2014.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

Bahrain was one of 11 Arab states which counted a majority of female university graduates in science and engineering in 2014. Women accounted for 66% of graduates in natural sciences, 28% of those in engineering and 77% of those in health and welfare. It is harder to judge the contribution of women to research, as the data for 2013 only cover the higher education sector.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

### Trends in research output

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Trends in research output")]

In 2014, Bahraini scientists published 155 articles in internationally catalogued journals, according to Thomson Reuters' Web of Science (Science Citation Index Expanded). This corresponds to 15 articles per million inhabitants, compared to a global average of 176 per million inhabitants in 2013. Scientific output has risen slowly from 93 articles in 2005 and remains modest. By 2014, only Mauritania and Palestine had a smaller output in this database among Arab states.[[296]](#cite_note-298)[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

Between 2008 and 2014, Bahraini scientists collaborated most with their peers from Saudi Arabia (137 articles), followed by Egypt (101), the Britain (93), the America (89) and Tunisia (75).[[293]](#cite_note-:0-295)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Bahrain](/wiki/Demographics_of_Bahrain "Demographics of Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Joining_in_prayer_-_Flickr_-_Al_Jazeera_English.jpg/250px-Joining_in_prayer_-_Flickr_-_Al_Jazeera_English.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joining_in_prayer_-_Flickr_-_Al_Jazeera_English.jpg)

Bahrainis observing public prayers in Manama

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Manama_Souq_%28Traditional_marketplace%29.jpg/250px-Manama_Souq_%28Traditional_marketplace%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manama_Souq_(Traditional_marketplace).jpg)

The Manama Souq on Bahrain National Day

In 2010, Bahrain's population was 1.2 million, of which 568,399 were Bahraini and 666,172 were non-nationals.[[297]](#cite_note-2010-census-299) It had risen from 1.05 million (517,368 non-nationals) in 2007, the year when Bahrain's population crossed the one million mark.[[298]](#cite_note-Ref_2008c-300) Though a majority of the population is Middle Eastern, a sizeable number of people from South Asia live in the country. In 2008, approximately 290,000 [Indian nationals](/wiki/Indian_diaspora "Indian diaspora") lived in Bahrain, making them the single largest expatriate community in the country, the majority from the south Indian state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala").[[299]](#cite_note-Ref_2008d-301)[[300]](#cite_note-IndEmb-302)

Bahrain is the fourth [most densely populated sovereign state in the world](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_by_population_density "List of sovereign states and dependent territories by population density") with a population density of 1,646 people per km2 in 2010.[[297]](#cite_note-2010-census-299) The only sovereign states with larger population densities are [city states](/wiki/City_states "City states"). Much of this population is concentrated in the north of the country with the [Southern Governorate](/wiki/Southern_Governorate "Southern Governorate") being the least densely populated part.[[297]](#cite_note-2010-census-299) The north of the country is so urbanised that it is considered by some to be one large [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

Main articles: [Bahrani people](/wiki/Bahrani_people "Bahrani people") and [Ethnic, cultural and religious groups of Bahrain](/wiki/Ethnic,_cultural_and_religious_groups_of_Bahrain "Ethnic, cultural and religious groups of Bahrain")

Bahraini people are ethnically diverse.

Shia Bahrainis are divided into two main ethnic groups: [Baharna](/wiki/Baharna "Baharna") and [Ajam](/wiki/Ajam_of_Bahrain "Ajam of Bahrain"). The [Baharna](/wiki/Baharna "Baharna") are ethnically [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab"), and the [Ajam](/wiki/Ajam_of_Bahrain "Ajam of Bahrain") are of [Persian](/wiki/Persians "Persians") origin. Shia Persians form large communities in Manama and Muharraq. A small minority of Shia Bahrainis are ethnic Hasawis from [Al-Hasa](/wiki/Al-Ahsa_Oasis "Al-Ahsa Oasis").

Sunni Bahrainis are mainly divided into two main ethnic groups: Arabs (al Arab) and [Huwala](/wiki/Huwala "Huwala"). Sunni Arabs are the most influential ethnic group in Bahrain. They hold most government positions and the [Bahraini monarchy](/wiki/Al_Khalifa "Al Khalifa") are Sunni Arabs. Sunni Arabs have traditionally lived in areas such as Zallaq, Muharraq, [West Riffa](/wiki/Riffa "Riffa") and Hawar islands. The Huwala are descendants of Sunni Iranians; some of them are Sunni Persians,[[301]](#cite_note-mig-303)[[302]](#cite_note-304) while others Sunni Arabs.[[303]](#cite_note-305)[[304]](#cite_note-306) There are also Sunnis of [Baloch](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people") origin. Most African Bahrainis come from [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa") and have traditionally lived in Muharraq Island and Riffa.[[305]](#cite_note-307)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main articles: [Freedom of religion in Bahrain](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Bahrain "Freedom of religion in Bahrain"), [Muharram in Bahrain](/wiki/Muharram_in_Bahrain "Muharram in Bahrain"), [Islam in Bahrain](/wiki/Islam_in_Bahrain "Islam in Bahrain"), and [Hinduism in Bahrain](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Bahrain "Hinduism in Bahrain")

Religion in Bahrain (2020) by [Pew Research](/wiki/Pew_Research "Pew Research"):[[4]](#cite_note-pew-4)

1. [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Bahrain "Islam in Bahrain") (69.7%)
2. [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Bahrain "Christianity in Bahrain") (14.1%)
3. [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Arab_states "Hinduism in Arab states") (10.2%)
4. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_Middle_East "Buddhism in the Middle East") (3.10%)
5. [Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Bahrain "History of the Jews in Bahrain") (0.00%)
6. [Other](/wiki/Demographics_of_Bahrain#Religion "Demographics of Bahrain") (0.90%)
7. Unaffiliated (2.00%)

The state religion of Bahrain is [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Bahrain "Islam in Bahrain") and most Bahrainis are Muslim. The majority of Bahraini Muslims are [Sunni Muslims](/wiki/Sunni_Muslims "Sunni Muslims") with a recent estimate giving them a 51% majority and [Shia Muslims](/wiki/Shia_Muslims "Shia Muslims") with a 49% minority.[[306]](#cite_note-308) It was previously one of the three countries in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") in which Shia were the majority, the other two nations being Iraq and Iran, though the rise of Sunni migrants made Sunnis the majority.[[307]](#cite_note-BBCShia2-309) Unofficial sources estimate sectarian identification to be approximately 55% [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") and 45% [Shia](/wiki/Shia "Shia").[[308]](#cite_note-310)[[309]](#cite_note-311) Public surveys are rare in Bahrain, but the [US department of state](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State")'s report on religious freedom in Bahrain estimated that Shias constituted approximately 55% of Bahrain's citizen population in 2018.[[310]](#cite_note-312) The royal family and most Bahrani elites are Sunni.[[311]](#cite_note-Pollock2-313) The country's two Muslim communities are united on some issues, but disagree sharply on others.[[311]](#cite_note-Pollock2-313) Shia have often complained of being politically and economically discriminated against in Bahrain; as a result, most of the protestors in the [Bahraini uprising of 2011](/wiki/Bahraini_uprising_of_2011 "Bahraini uprising of 2011") were Shia.[[312]](#cite_note-314)[[313]](#cite_note-315)[[314]](#cite_note-316)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/National_Evangelical_Church_Manama.jpg/250px-National_Evangelical_Church_Manama.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Evangelical_Church_Manama.jpg)

National Evangelical Church, Manama

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Shrinathji_Temple.jpg/250px-Shrinathji_Temple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shrinathji_Temple.jpg)

The Shrinathji temple in Manama

[Christians in Bahrain](/wiki/Christianity_in_Bahrain "Christianity in Bahrain") make up about 14.5% of the population.[[297]](#cite_note-2010-census-299) There is a native [Christian community in Bahrain](/wiki/Christianity_in_Bahrain "Christianity in Bahrain"). Non-Muslim Bahraini residents numbered 367,683 per the 2010 census, most of whom are Christians.[[315]](#cite_note-2010Census-317) Expatriate Christians make up the majority of Christians in Bahrain, while native Christian Bahrainis (who hold Bahraini citizenship) make up a smaller community. Native Christians who hold Bahraini citizenship number approximately 1,000 persons.[[315]](#cite_note-2010Census-317) [Alees Samaan](/wiki/Alees_Samaan "Alees Samaan"), a former Bahraini ambassador to Britain is a native Christian. Bahrain also has a native [Jewish community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Bahrain "History of the Jews in Bahrain") numbering thirty-seven Bahraini citizens.[[316]](#cite_note-Yaar-318) Various sources cite Bahrain's native Jewish community as being from 36 to 50 people.[[317]](#cite_note-319) According to Bahraini writer [Nancy Khedouri](/wiki/Nancy_Khedouri "Nancy Khedouri"), the Jewish community of Bahrain is one of the youngest in the world, having its origins in the migration of a few families to the island from then-Iraq and then-Iran in the late 1880s.[[318]](#cite_note-320) [Houda Nonoo](/wiki/Houda_Nonoo "Houda Nonoo"), former ambassador to America, is Jewish. There is also a Hindu community on the island. They constitute the third largest religious group. The [Shrinathji temple](/wiki/Shrinathji_Temple,_Bahrain "Shrinathji Temple, Bahrain") located in old Manama is the oldest Hindu temple in the [GCC](/wiki/Gulf_Cooperation_Council "Gulf Cooperation Council") and the Arab world. It is over 200 years old and was built by the Thattai Hindu community in 1817.[[319]](#cite_note-321)

According to the 2001 census, 81.2% of Bahrain's population was Muslim, 10% were Christian, and 9.8% practised [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Arab_states "Hinduism in Arab states") or [other religions](/wiki/Demographics_of_Bahrain#Religion "Demographics of Bahrain").[[10]](#cite_note-CIA-10) The 2010 census records that the Muslim proportion had fallen to 70.2% (the 2010 census did not differentiate between the non-Muslim religions).[[297]](#cite_note-2010-census-299)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Languages")]

[Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") is the official language of Bahrain, though English is widely used.[[320]](#cite_note-BO-322) [Bahrani Arabic](/wiki/Bahrani_Arabic "Bahrani Arabic") is the most widely spoken dialect of the Arabic language and is different from standard Arabic, like all Arabic dialects. Arabic plays an important role in political life, as, according to Bahrain's constitution, an MP must be fluent in Arabic to stand for parliament.[[321]](#cite_note-323)

[Balochi](/wiki/Balochi_language "Balochi language") is the second largest and widely spoken language in Bahrain.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Baloch are fluent in Arabic and Balochi. Among the Bahraini and non-Bahraini population, many people speak [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), the official language of Iran, or [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), an official language in Pakistan and a regional language in India.[[320]](#cite_note-BO-322)

[Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") is also widely spoken in the [Nepalese workers and Gurkha Soldiers](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") community. [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), [Bangla](/wiki/Bangla_language "Bangla language") and [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") are spoken among significant Indian communities.[[320]](#cite_note-BO-322) All commercial institutions and road signs are [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual_sign "Bilingual sign"), displaying both English and Arabic.[[322]](#cite_note-324)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Bahrain](/wiki/Education_in_Bahrain "Education in Bahrain")

Bahrain boasts a diverse education system that includes a variety of private schools offering international curricula. Among these are British, American, and other international schools, catering to a wide range of educational needs.

See also: [List of universities in Bahrain](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Bahrain "List of universities in Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/University_students_Bahrain.jpg/250px-University_students_Bahrain.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_students_Bahrain.jpg)

Female students at the [University of Bahrain](/wiki/University_of_Bahrain "University of Bahrain") dressed in traditional garb

Education is compulsory for children between the ages of 6 and 14.[[323]](#cite_note-325) Education is free for Bahraini citizens in [government schools](/wiki/State_school "State school"), with the Bahraini [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Bahrain) "Ministry of Education (Bahrain)") providing free textbooks. [Coeducation](/wiki/Coeducation "Coeducation") is not used in government schools, with boys and girls segregated into separate schools.[[324]](#cite_note-326)

At the beginning of the 20th century, Qur'anic schools (*[Kuttab](/wiki/Kuttab "Kuttab")*) were the only form of education in Bahrain.[[325]](#cite_note-MoE-327) They were traditional schools aimed at teaching children and youth the reading of the [Qur'an](/wiki/Qur%27an "Qur'an"). After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Bahrain became open to western influences, and a demand for modern educational institutions appeared. 1919 marked the beginning of modern government school system in Bahrain when the Al-Hidaya Al-Khalifia School for boys opened in [Muharraq](/wiki/Muharraq "Muharraq").[[325]](#cite_note-MoE-327) In 1926, the Education Committee opened the second government school for boys in Manama, and in 1928 the first government school for girls was opened in Muharraq.[[325]](#cite_note-MoE-327) In 2011, there were 126,981 students studying in government schools.[[326]](#cite_note-328)

In 2004, King [Hamad ibn Isa Al Khalifa](/wiki/Hamad_ibn_Isa_Al_Khalifa "Hamad ibn Isa Al Khalifa") introduced the "King Hamad Schools of Future" project that uses Information Communication Technology to support [K–12 education](/wiki/K%E2%80%9312_education "K–12 education") in Bahrain.[[327]](#cite_note-329) The project's objective is to connect all schools within the kingdom with the Internet.[[328]](#cite_note-330) In addition to British intermediate schools, the island is served by the [Bahrain School](/wiki/Bahrain_School "Bahrain School") (BS). The BS is an [American Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") school that provides a K–12 curriculum including [International Baccalaureate](/wiki/International_Baccalaureate "International Baccalaureate") offerings. There are also international schools that offer either the [IB Diploma Programme](/wiki/IB_Diploma_Programme "IB Diploma Programme") or Britain's [A-Levels](/wiki/A-Levels "A-Levels").

Bahrain also encourages institutions of higher learning, drawing on expatriate talent and the increasing pool of Bahrain nationals returning from abroad with advanced degrees. The [University of Bahrain](/wiki/University_of_Bahrain "University of Bahrain") was established for standard undergraduate and graduate study, and the [King Abdulaziz University College of Health Sciences](/wiki/King_Abdulaziz_University_College_of_Health_Sciences "King Abdulaziz University College of Health Sciences"), operating under the direction of the Ministry of Health, trains [physicians](/wiki/Physician "Physician"), [nurses](/wiki/Nursing "Nursing"), [pharmacists](/wiki/Pharmacist "Pharmacist") and [paramedics](/wiki/Paramedic "Paramedic"). The 2001 National Action Charter paved the way for the formation of international universities such as the [Ahlia University](/wiki/Ahlia_University "Ahlia University") in Manama and [University College of Bahrain](/wiki/University_College_of_Bahrain "University College of Bahrain") in [Saar](/wiki/Saar,_Bahrain "Saar, Bahrain").

The Royal University for Women (RUW), established in 2005, was the first purpose-built, international university in Bahrain dedicated solely to educating women. The [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London") External has appointed MCG (Management Consultancy Group) as the regional representative office in Bahrain for distance learning programmes.[[329]](#cite_note-331) MCG is one of the oldest international institutes in the country. Institutes have also opened which educate South Asian students, such as the [Pakistan Urdu School, Bahrain](/w/index.php?title=Pakistan_Urdu_School,_Bahrain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pakistan Urdu School, Bahrain (page does not exist)") and the [Indian School, Bahrain](/wiki/Indian_School,_Bahrain "Indian School, Bahrain").

A few prominent institutions are the [American University of Bahrain](/wiki/American_University_of_Bahrain "American University of Bahrain") established in 2019,[[330]](#cite_note-332) the [Bahrain Institute of Banking and Finance](/wiki/Bahrain_Institute_of_Banking_and_Finance "Bahrain Institute of Banking and Finance"), the Ernst & Young Training Institute, and the [Birla Institute of Technology International Centre](/wiki/Birla_Institute_of_Technology_International_Centre "Birla Institute of Technology International Centre"). In 2004, the [Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Surgeons_in_Ireland "Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland") (RCSI) set up a constituent [medical university](/wiki/RCSI-Bahrain "RCSI-Bahrain") in the country. In addition to the [Arabian Gulf University](/wiki/Arabian_Gulf_University "Arabian Gulf University"), [AMA International University](/wiki/AMA_International_University "AMA International University") and the [College of Health Sciences](/wiki/College_of_Health_Sciences,_Bahrain "College of Health Sciences, Bahrain"), these are the only medical schools in Bahrain.

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Health")]

Main articles: [Health in Bahrain](/wiki/Health_in_Bahrain "Health in Bahrain") and [Healthcare in Bahrain](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Bahrain "Healthcare in Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/IHB_Ambulance.jpg/250px-IHB_Ambulance.jpg)](/wiki/File:IHB_Ambulance.jpg)

An ambulance at the [International Hospital of Bahrain](/wiki/International_Hospital_of_Bahrain "International Hospital of Bahrain")

Bahrain has a [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") system, dating back to 1960.[[331]](#cite_note-333) Government-provided health care is free to Bahraini citizens and heavily subsidised for non-Bahrainis. Healthcare expenditure accounted for 4.5% of Bahrain's GDP, according to the [World Health Organisation](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization"). Bahraini physicians and nurses form a majority of the country's workforce in the health sector, unlike neighbouring Gulf states.[[332]](#cite_note-334)

The first [hospital](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital") in Bahrain was the [American Mission Hospital](/wiki/American_Mission_Hospital "American Mission Hospital"), which opened in 1893 as a dispensary.[[333]](#cite_note-335) The first public hospital, and also [tertiary hospital](/wiki/Tertiary_referral_hospital "Tertiary referral hospital"), to open in Bahrain was the [Salmaniya Medical Complex](/wiki/Salmaniya_Medical_Complex "Salmaniya Medical Complex"), in the [Salmaniya](/wiki/Salmaniya "Salmaniya") district of Manama, in 1957.[[334]](#cite_note-336) Private hospitals are also present throughout the country, such as the [International Hospital of Bahrain](/wiki/International_Hospital_of_Bahrain "International Hospital of Bahrain").

The [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") in Bahrain is 73 for males and 76 for females. Compared to many countries in the region, the prevalence of [AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS") and [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV") is relatively low.[[335]](#cite_note-UNDP-337) [Malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") and [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") (TB) do not constitute major problems in Bahrain as neither disease is indigenous to the country. As a result, cases of malaria and TB have declined in recent decades with cases of contractions amongst Bahraini nationals becoming rare.[[335]](#cite_note-UNDP-337) The Ministry of Health sponsors regular vaccination campaigns against TB and other diseases such as [hepatitis B](/wiki/Hepatitis_B "Hepatitis B").[[335]](#cite_note-UNDP-337)[[336]](#cite_note-338)

Bahrain has an [obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity") epidemic, with 28.9% of all males and 38.2% of all females are classified as obese in 2012.[[337]](#cite_note-339) In 2010, Bahrain had one of the highest prevalence of [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes_mellitus "Diabetes mellitus") in the world, at 5th place. In 2010, more than 15% of the Bahraini population were affected by diabetes, and they account for 5% of deaths in Bahrain.[[338]](#cite_note-340) [Cardiovascular diseases](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease") account for 32% of all deaths in Bahrain, being the number one cause of death in the country (the second being [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer")).[[339]](#cite_note-341) [Sickle-cell anaemia](/wiki/Sickle-cell_disease "Sickle-cell disease") and [thalassaemia](/wiki/Thalassaemia "Thalassaemia") are prevalent in Bahrain. 18% of Bahrainis are carriers of sickle-cell anaemia. 24% are carriers of thalassaemia.[[340]](#cite_note-342)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Bahrain](/wiki/Culture_of_Bahrain "Culture of Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Isa_Bin_Ali_House.jpg/250px-Isa_Bin_Ali_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Isa_Bin_Ali_House.jpg)

The [Isa ibn Ali Al Khalifa](/wiki/Isa_ibn_Ali_Al_Khalifa "Isa ibn Ali Al Khalifa") house is an example of traditional architecture in Bahrain.

Islam is the main religion, and Bahrainis are known for their tolerance towards the practice of other faiths.[[341]](#cite_note-343) Intermarriages between Bahrainis and expatriates are not uncommon—there are many Filipino Bahrainis like Filipino child actress [Mona Marbella Al-Alawi](/wiki/Mona_Louise_Rey "Mona Louise Rey").[[342]](#cite_note-344)

Rules regarding female attire are generally relaxed compared to regional neighbours; the traditional attire of women usually include the [hijab](/wiki/Hijab "Hijab") or the [abaya](/wiki/Abaya "Abaya").[[143]](#cite_note-Britannica-145) Although the traditional male attire is the [thobe](/wiki/Thawb "Thawb"), which also includes traditional headdresses such as the [keffiyeh](/wiki/Keffiyeh "Keffiyeh"), [ghutra](/wiki/Keffiyeh "Keffiyeh") and [agal](/wiki/Agal_(accessory) "Agal (accessory)"), Western clothing is common in the country.[[143]](#cite_note-Britannica-145)

Although Bahrain legalised [homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") in 1976, many homosexuals have since been arrested, often for violating broadly written laws against public immorality and public indecency.[[343]](#cite_note-345)[[344]](#cite_note-346)[[345]](#cite_note-347)

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Art")]

Main article: [Bahraini art](/wiki/Bahraini_art "Bahraini art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Bahrain_Pottery_Creator.jpg/250px-Bahrain_Pottery_Creator.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bahrain_Pottery_Creator.jpg)

An artisan making pottery using the traditional mud and water mixture on a revolving wheel

The modern art movement in the country officially emerged in the 1950s, culminating in the establishment of an art society. [Expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism") and [surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), as well as [calligraphic art](/wiki/Calligraphy "Calligraphy") are the popular forms of art in the country. [Abstract expressionism](/wiki/Abstract_expressionism "Abstract expressionism") has gained popularity in recent decades.[[346]](#cite_note-TGE-348) [Pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery")-making and [textile](/wiki/Textile "Textile")-weaving are also popular products that were widely made in Bahraini villages.[[346]](#cite_note-TGE-348) Arabic calligraphy grew in popularity as the [Bahraini government](/wiki/Bahraini_government "Bahraini government") was an active patron in [Muslim art](/wiki/Muslim_art "Muslim art"), culminating in the establishment of a Muslim museum, [Beit Al Quran](/wiki/Beit_Al_Quran "Beit Al Quran").[[346]](#cite_note-TGE-348)

The [Bahrain National Museum](/wiki/Bahrain_National_Museum "Bahrain National Museum") houses a permanent [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art") exhibition.[[347]](#cite_note-349) The annual Spring of Culture[[348]](#cite_note-350) festival run by the Bahrain Authority for Culture and Antiquities[[349]](#cite_note-351) has become a popular event promoting performance arts in the kingdom. The [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture") of Bahrain is similar to that of its neighbours in the Persian Gulf. The [wind tower](/wiki/Windcatcher "Windcatcher"), which generates natural ventilation in a house, is a common sight on old buildings, particularly in the old districts of Manama and [Muharraq](/wiki/Muharraq "Muharraq").[[350]](#cite_note-Ali-352)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main article: [Literature of Bahrain](/wiki/Literature_of_Bahrain "Literature of Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/National_Library_at_ICC.jpg/250px-National_Library_at_ICC.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Library_at_ICC.jpg)

The [National Library of Bahrain at Isa Cultural Centre](/wiki/National_Library_of_Bahrain_at_Isa_Cultural_Centre "National Library of Bahrain at Isa Cultural Centre")

Literature retains a strong tradition in the country; most traditional writers and poets write in the [classical Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic") style. In recent years, the number of younger poets influenced by [western literature](/wiki/Western_literature "Western literature") are rising, most writing in [free verse](/wiki/Free_verse "Free verse") and often including political or personal content.[[351]](#cite_note-EC-353) [Ali Al Shargawi](/wiki/Ali_Al_Shargawi "Ali Al Shargawi"), a decorated longtime poet, was described in 2011 by *Al Shorfa* as the literary icon of Bahrain.[[352]](#cite_note-AS-354)

In literature, Bahrain was the site of the ancient land of [Dilmun](/wiki/Dilmun "Dilmun") mentioned in the [Epic of Gilgamesh](/wiki/Epic_of_Gilgamesh "Epic of Gilgamesh"). Legend also states that it was the location of the [Garden of Eden](/wiki/Garden_of_Eden "Garden of Eden").[[353]](#cite_note-NYT-355)[[354]](#cite_note-LAT-356)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media of Bahrain](/wiki/Media_of_Bahrain "Media of Bahrain")

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of Bahrain](/wiki/Music_of_Bahrain "Music of Bahrain")

The music style in Bahrain is similar to that of its neighbours. The [Khaliji](/wiki/Khaliji_(music) "Khaliji (music)") style of music, which is [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), is popular in the country. The [sawt](/wiki/Sawt_(music) "Sawt (music)") style of music, which involves a complex form of urban music, performed by an [Oud](/wiki/Oud "Oud") (plucked lute), a [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and [mirwas](/wiki/Mirwas "Mirwas") (a drum), is also popular in Bahrain.[[355]](#cite_note-AUC-357) [Ali Bahar](/wiki/Ali_Bahar "Ali Bahar") was one of the most famous singers in Bahrain. He performed his music with his Band *Al-Ekhwa* (*The Brothers*). Bahrain was also the site of the first [recording studio](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio") amongst the Persian Gulf states.[[355]](#cite_note-AUC-357)

### Entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Entertainment")]

See also: [Category:Entertainment in Bahrain](/wiki/Category:Entertainment_in_Bahrain "Category:Entertainment in Bahrain")

With regards to cultural and tourism activities, the Ministry of Culture[[356]](#cite_note-358) organises a number of annual festivals. such as the Spring of Culture in March and April, the [Bahrain Summer Festival](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain_Summer_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bahrain Summer Festival (page does not exist)") and Ta'a Al-Shabab from August to September, and the Bahrain International Music Festival in October which features musical and theatrical performances, lectures, and much more.

As for cultural sites, residents, visitors, and tourists can re-live history through Bahrain's many historical sites.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bahrain&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sport in Bahrain](/wiki/Sport_in_Bahrain "Sport in Bahrain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Mixed_Martial_Arts_event_in_Bahrain.jpg/250px-Mixed_Martial_Arts_event_in_Bahrain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mixed_Martial_Arts_event_in_Bahrain.jpg)

[Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Mixed_Martial_Arts "Mixed Martial Arts") hosted by BRAVE Combat Federation event in Bahrain

Bahrain is the first nation other than America to host [International Mixed Martial Arts Federation](/wiki/International_Mixed_Martial_Arts_Federation "International Mixed Martial Arts Federation") World Championships of Amateur MMA in partnership with [Brave Combat Federation](/wiki/Brave_Combat_Federation "Brave Combat Federation").[[357]](#cite_note-359) Bahrain have recorded an influx in global athletes visiting the nation for Mixed Martial Arts training during 2017.[[358]](#cite_note-360) [Brave Combat Federation](/wiki/Brave_Combat_Federation "Brave Combat Federation") is a Bahrain-based Mixed Martial Arts promotion that has hosted events in 30 nations which is a record for hosting events in most number of nations by an MMA promotion.[[359]](#cite_note-361)

Bahrain MMA Federation (BMMAF) has been set up under the patronage of Sheikh [Khalid bin Hamad Al Khalifa](/wiki/Khalid_bin_Hamad_Al_Khalifa "Khalid bin Hamad Al Khalifa") and the jurisdiction of the Sports Minister, Sheikh [Nasser bin Hamad Al Khalifa](/wiki/Nasser_bin_Hamad_Al_Khalifa "Nasser bin Hamad Al Khalifa").[[360]](#cite_note-362) The development of MMA in the nation is convened through KHK MMA, which owns [Brave Combat Federation](/wiki/Brave_Combat_Federation "Brave Combat Federation") which is the largest Mixed Martial Arts promotion in the Middle East.[[361]](#cite_note-363) Bahrain will be hosting Amateur World Championships 2017 in association with [International Mixed Martial Arts Federation](/wiki/International_Mixed_Martial_Arts_Federation "International Mixed Martial Arts Federation"). Bahrain will be the first Asian and [Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") country to host the amateur MMA championship.[[362]](#cite_note-364) Bahrain is also home to KHK [MMA](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") Fight Team, that facilitates training for some of the prominent talent in [Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Mixed_Martial_Arts "Mixed Martial Arts") in the world who compete in BRAVE Combat Federation, [PFL](/wiki/Professional_Fighters_League "Professional Fighters League"), and [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC").[[363]](#cite_note-365)

In 2018, [Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") was introduced in Bahrain under the initiative of KHK Sports and Exelon.[[364]](#cite_note-366) Bahrain Premier League 2018 comprised six franchise squads of 13 resident cricketers competing in the T20 format. The teams were SRam MRam Falcons, Kalaam Knight-Riders, Intex Lions, Bahrain Super Giants, Four Square Challengers and Awan Warriors.[[365]](#cite_note-367)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/SocceroosvsBahrain.jpg/250px-SocceroosvsBahrain.jpg)](/wiki/File:SocceroosvsBahrain.jpg)

The Bahrain national football team playing [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") in June 2009, in a World Cup qualifier

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is also a popular sport in Bahrain.[[366]](#cite_note-autogenerated6-368) [Bahrain's national football team](/wiki/Bahrain_national_football_team "Bahrain national football team") has competed multiple times at the [Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup"), [Arab Nations Cup](/wiki/Arab_Nations_Cup "Arab Nations Cup") and played in the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") qualifiers, though it has never qualified for the World Cup.[[367]](#cite_note-369) Bahrain's national football team won the [West Asian Football Federation](/wiki/West_Asian_Football_Federation "West Asian Football Federation") cup and the [Arabian Gulf Cup](/wiki/Arabian_Gulf_Cup "Arabian Gulf Cup") in 2019.[[368]](#cite_note-370)[[369]](#cite_note-371) Both the cups came under the helm of Helio Sousa who is the manager of the nation's national football team. Bahrain has its own [top-tier](/wiki/Ranking "Ranking") domestic professional [football league](/wiki/Geography_of_association_football "Geography of association football"), the [Bahraini Premier League](/wiki/Bahraini_Premier_League "Bahraini Premier League"). On 3 August 2020, the Kingdom of Bahrain bought a minority stake in the [Paris F.C.](/wiki/Paris_FC "Paris FC"), a team that plays in France's second tier. Bahrain's entry into the soccer club spurred criticism that the country is trying to whitewash its [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") record and this is another way of buying influence in Europe.[[370]](#cite_note-372)

[Basketball](/wiki/Sport_in_Bahrain#Basketball "Sport in Bahrain"), [rugby](/wiki/Sport_in_Bahrain#Rugby "Sport in Bahrain") and [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") are also widely popular in the country.[[366]](#cite_note-autogenerated6-368) The government of Bahrain also sponsors a [UCI WorldTeam](/wiki/UCI_WorldTeam "UCI WorldTeam") cycling team, [Bahrain Victorius](/wiki/Bahrain%E2%80%93Merida_Pro_Cycling_Team "Bahrain–Merida Pro Cycling Team"), which participated in the [2017 Tour de France](/wiki/2017_Tour_de_France "2017 Tour de France").[[371]](#cite_note-373)[[372]](#cite_note-374)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/2007_Bahrain_GP_podium.jpg/250px-2007_Bahrain_GP_podium.jpg)](/wiki/File:2007_Bahrain_GP_podium.jpg)

The podium ceremony at the [2007 Bahrain Grand Prix](/wiki/2007_Bahrain_Grand_Prix "2007 Bahrain Grand Prix")

Bahrain has a [Formula One race track](/wiki/Bahrain_International_Circuit "Bahrain International Circuit"), which hosted the inaugural [Gulf Air](/wiki/Gulf_Air "Gulf Air") [Bahrain Grand Prix](/wiki/Bahrain_Grand_Prix "Bahrain Grand Prix") on 4 April 2004, the first in an Arab country. This was followed by the Bahrain Grand Prix in 2005. Bahrain hosted the opening Grand Prix of the 2006 season on 12 March of that year. Both the above races were won by [Fernando Alonso](/wiki/Fernando_Alonso "Fernando Alonso") of [Renault](/wiki/Renault "Renault"). The race has since been hosted annually, except for [2011](/wiki/2011_Bahrain_Grand_Prix "2011 Bahrain Grand Prix") when it was cancelled due to [ongoing anti-government protests](/wiki/Bahraini_uprising_of_2011 "Bahraini uprising of 2011").[[373]](#cite_note-autosport89525-375) The [2012](/wiki/2012_Bahrain_Grand_Prix "2012 Bahrain Grand Prix") race occurred despite concerns of the safety of the teams and the ongoing [protests](/wiki/2012_Bahrain_Grand_Prix_protests "2012 Bahrain Grand Prix protests") in the country.[[374]](#cite_note-376) The decision to hold the race despite ongoing protests and violence[[375]](#cite_note-race_on-377) has been described as "controversial" by [Al Jazeera English](/wiki/Al_Jazeera_English "Al Jazeera English"),[[376]](#cite_note-378) [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"),[[377]](#cite_note-379) [AFP](/wiki/Agence_France-Presse "Agence France-Presse")[[378]](#cite_note-380) and [Sky News](/wiki/Sky_News "Sky News").[[379]](#cite_note-381) *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* named it "one of the most controversial in the history of the sport".[[380]](#cite_note-382)

In 2006, Bahrain also hosted its inaugural Australian [V8 Supercar](/wiki/Supercars_Championship "Supercars Championship") event dubbed the "*Desert 400*". The V8s returned every November to the [Sakhir](/wiki/Sakhir "Sakhir") circuit until 2010, in which it was the second event of the series. The series has not returned since. The [Bahrain International Circuit](/wiki/Bahrain_International_Circuit "Bahrain International Circuit") also features a full-length [dragstrip](/wiki/Dragstrip "Dragstrip") where the Bahrain Drag Racing Club has organised invitational events featuring some of Europe's top drag racing teams to try to raise the profile of the sport in the Middle East.[[381]](#cite_note-383)

On 10 June 2024, the Bahrain Olympic Academy received the [Athena](/wiki/Athena "Athena") honorary distinction for its role in aiding and supporting advancement of sports in its region. The honorary medal was presented to his highness Shaikh [Khalid bin Hamad Al Khalifa](/wiki/Khalid_bin_Hamad_Al_Khalifa "Khalid bin Hamad Al Khalifa") by Isidoros Kouvelos, President of [IOA](/wiki/International_Olympic_Academy "International Olympic Academy").[[382]](#cite_note-384)[[383]](#cite_note-385)
